turns.content: I'm exploring Rust for a proof-of-concept in embedded firmware. I've read about attributes like #[link_name] and #[link_section], but I'm not quite sure how they work or when to use them.

Ah, good question! Those are some advanced features of Rust's `#[repr(C)]` and `#[link_name = "c_func_name"]` attributes. When you want your Rust code to interface with C libraries or embedded hardware, these can be very handy. For example, if you have a Rust ISR handler but need it to match the name expected by your C runtime, you'd use `#[link_name]`. It's like renaming a file in an operating system—just so that when the linker looks for symbols, it finds them under the right name.
Hey, I'm learning Rust. Can you explain how to link Rust functions with C symbols in embedded firmware?

Sure! When working on embedded firmware, sometimes you want your Rust-generated code to interoperate with existing C libraries or hardware drivers. One way to do this is by using the `#[link_name]` attribute. It allows you to specify a different name for exported functions when they're linked into the final executable.
